full_text: <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> So tonight is Maundy Thursday. The word Maundy is a strange word for us in the 21st century because it comes from the Latin word mandatum, which means commandment.<|14.70|> <|14.70|> So today is technically called Commandment Thursday.<|18.58|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The commandment, of course, is Jesus' command to the disciples to love one another.<|6.04|> <|7.22|> But his command is not just to love one another, full stop, but to love one another as Jesus has loved them.<|16.58|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And Jesus doesn't love his disciples in an abstract way.<|5.06|> <|6.34|> Jesus loves them in a concrete way.<|8.80|> <|10.24|> Jesus doesn't merely say the words, but he shows the disciples what love actually looks like.<|17.60|> <|18.64|> And tonight, Jesus' love looks like a towel and a basin.<|25.80|> <|25.80|> Amen.<|26.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> He is introducing the disciples to a pattern of life, which I want to call the way of the towel.<|7.30|> <|10.74|> Now, this way of the towel, I think, is sometimes overshadowed by Jesus' institution of the Eucharist.<|20.22|> <|20.22|> Because if you keep reading in the story, the Eucharist follows right after this.<|26.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> But before there was the bread and the cup, there was the towel and the basin.<|6.92|> <|8.42|> So if we skip over the foot washing and go straight to the Eucharist, then we are in danger of partaking in the Eucharist as an isolated and empty practice.<|18.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> If we skip the foot washing, we will miss the fact that acts of service to the neighbor<|5.50|> <|5.50|> and partaking of the body and blood of Christ go hand in hand.<|10.84|> <|12.66|> Acts of service without being grounded in Christ-like love is simply secular humanism.<|19.38|> <|20.68|> The orientation and attitude from which we love and serve our neighbors matters.<|25.68|> <|26.24|> It matters.<|26.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And likewise, partaking in the Christian sacraments without being formed and challenged by the practice of love and encounter with our neighbor is either simply mysticism or empty ritual.<|15.56|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Neither of these practices on their own is the full expression of our Christian life.<|5.36|> <|6.52|> The former is content without form, and the latter is form without content.<|13.06|> <|14.02|> So tonight we have the chance as a Christian community to practice the act of foot washing<|19.80|> <|19.80|> first, followed by the Eucharist.<|23.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And it's a rare opportunity to live out these two interconnected aspects of the Christian life in one liturgical service,<|9.18|> <|9.54|> each of which gives meaning to the other.<|12.48|> <|14.36|> So tonight I want to focus on this way of the towel.<|18.02|> <|19.74|> Jesus' act of foot washing isn't merely ceremonial, nor is it ritual.<|25.18|> <|25.18|> It's ritual.<|26.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> just as we don't believe the Eucharist is merely ceremonial or simply ritual.<|7.00|> <|7.00|> The way of the towel is lowly, messy, concrete, awkward work,<|14.00|> <|14.00|> which requires a real encounter with another human being<|19.00|> <|19.00|> in the stark, messy, uncomfortable reality which is our human life.<|26.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> As such, foot washing was a task that no one in the household wanted to do.<|6.28|> <|6.28|> So it was reserved for the lowest servant.<|11.82|> <|11.82|> And it was deemed so menial that many Jewish slaves were exempt from it.<|17.08|> <|17.08|> And it was delegated even further down to the lowest Gentile slave.<|22.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> But in those days, foot washing was a necessary task, since guests would arrive for a meal<|6.68|> <|6.68|> with dirty, dusty, bloody, blistered, dung-caked feet after a long day's journey.<|13.78|> <|15.18|> Most people walked barefoot in those days, since only those with money could afford sandals.<|21.68|> <|21.68|> Amen.<|22.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> So not only was foot washing an act of hospitality to one's guests, it was also practically important to keep the floors clean, free of dust, blood, and other foot infections.<|15.50|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> So to have Jesus, the long-awaited Messiah,<|2.90|> <|3.18|> proclaimed king of the Jews,<|4.64|> <|5.06|> rabbi, teacher, and healer,<|7.18|> <|8.02|> wash the disciples' feet was not only shocking,<|11.08|> <|12.20|> it actually would have been considered deeply offensive.<|15.40|> <|15.40|> Thank you.<|16.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And this helps us understand why Peter is both shocked and offended at Jesus' action.<|6.88|> <|7.88|> Lord, you're going to wash my feet.<|10.96|> <|12.60|> Jesus replies, you do not realize now what I'm doing, but later you will understand.<|18.54|> <|19.92|> But Peter is adamant. He doubles down.<|22.38|> <|22.54|> Lord, you will never wash my feet.<|25.26|> <|25.26|> Amen.<|26.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> But Jesus responds patiently again, unless I wash you, you have no share with me.<|7.46|> <|9.20|> So it isn't until after Jesus tells Peter of the consequences for refusing to let Jesus wash his feet that Peter complies.<|18.02|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> But even then, Peter still tries to reframe the action into one of bathing and purification.<|6.88|> <|7.94|> He says, Lord, not my feet only, but also my hands and my head.<|11.22|> <|13.04|> But Jesus will not allow Peter to make Jesus' actions more palatable to him.<|18.16|> <|19.90|> Jesus says, one who has bathed does not need to wash except for the feet, but is entirely clean.<|26.04|> <|26.04|> Amen.<|26.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Bottom line, Peter is going to have to accept the reality that Jesus, his Lord and teacher, is going to wash his feet.<|8.32|> <|8.82|> No matter how shocking, embarrassing, and offensive the action is to him.<|13.84|> <|15.46|> So after Jesus is done, he says to the disciples,<|18.56|> <|19.76|> Do you know what I have done for you?<|22.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> You call me teacher and Lord, and you're right, for that is what I am.<|4.92|> <|5.84|> So if I, your Lord and teacher, have washed your feet, you also ought to wash one another's feet.<|12.80|> <|13.84|> For I have set the example that you should do as I have done to you.<|17.88|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Thank you.<|1.20|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And so with this statement, Jesus is essentially telling his disciples that believing in him isn't enough.<|8.00|> <|9.54|> Believing is a necessary, but it's not a sufficient condition for the fullness of the Christian life.<|16.36|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Discipleship isn't simply about personal belief and theological knowledge, no matter how advanced.<|6.98|> <|8.20|> Being a disciple requires us to operationalize our personal belief and theological knowledge<|14.68|> <|14.68|> into a pattern of life.<|16.64|> <|17.92|> A pattern of life which is about being like Jesus and needing to imitate him.<|23.74|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And imitation isn't just through teaching and preaching or healing.<|4.74|> <|5.90|> Imitation involves humble acts of service that cannot be complicated or misunderstood<|11.48|> <|11.48|> by doing them from a place of social status or power.<|16.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> In the act of foot washing, Christ sets aside his status as Lord and teacher and shows the<|6.04|> <|6.04|> disciples that being a follower of Christ means that no act of love is beneath them.<|12.78|> <|14.28|> The way of the towel is service over status.<|18.70|> <|19.90|> It's humility over power.<|21.94|> <|22.74|> And it's love over pride.<|24.82|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Thank you.<|1.28|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> So I'd like to close this evening by asking us to think about Jesus' command to the disciples<|7.06|> <|7.06|> to wash one another's feet as a form of their commissioning.<|13.46|> <|14.86|> With this demonstration of service, humility, and love,<|18.40|> <|19.46|> Jesus is commissioning his disciples for the life that awaits them.<|23.66|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Similar to the way we hear the Great Commission,<|3.28|> <|3.54|> go ye into all the world and preach the gospel to every nation.<|7.10|> <|9.16|> Tonight let us hear these words,<|11.12|> <|12.12|> so if I, your Lord and teacher, have washed your feet,<|15.00|> <|15.72|> you also ought to wash one another's feet.<|18.14|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> and<|1.06|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Let us hear these words as a form of commissioning for us to follow as well.<|6.04|> <|7.44|> It is a commission from Jesus to embrace the way of the towel.<|12.06|> <|14.40|> When you come forward in just a few minutes, I invite you to engage in the foot washing<|18.88|> <|18.88|> as a personal and public act of rededicating yourself to following the way of the towel.<|27.84|> <|27.84|> Amen.<|28.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Do not think of yourself as merely engaging in an ancient ritual, however embarrassing, that's long been since discarded.<|10.84|> <|12.14|> Rather, enter into it as a way of being and as a commitment to a pattern of living that Christ invites us into.<|22.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Thank you.<|1.50|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Be willing to be a Christian who will kneel down,<|6.00|> <|7.18|> confront people and things you would rather not see,<|10.98|> <|11.82|> and be willing to get your hands dirty<|14.06|> <|14.06|> in order to be a witness to Christ's love.<|18.16|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Thank you.<|1.82|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.44|> And finally, the next time someone asks you what love looks like,<|5.56|> <|6.36|> here's your answer.<|7.66|> <|9.28|> It looks like a towel and a basin.<|12.26|> <|13.94|> Tomorrow, it'll look like a cross.<|16.60|> <|16.60|> Amen.<|18.44|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> - Amen.<|0.84|><|endoftext|>

<|14.36|> So tonight I want to focus on this way of the towel.<|18.02|>

<|15.18|> Most people walked barefoot in those days, since only those with money could afford sandals.<|21.68|>

<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> So not only was foot washing an act of hospitality to one's guests, it was also practically important to keep the floors clean, free of dust, blood, and other foot infections.<|15.50|><|endoftext|>

<|7.88|> Lord, you're going to wash my feet.<|10.96|>
<|12.60|> Jesus replies, you do not realize now what I'm doing, but later you will understand.<|18.54|>

<|19.90|> Jesus says, one who has bathed does not need to wash except for the feet, but is entirely clean.<|26.04|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Bottom line, Peter is going to have to accept the reality that Jesus, his Lord and teacher, is going to wash his feet.<|8.32|>

<|5.84|> So if I, your Lord and teacher, have washed your feet, you also ought to wash one another's feet.<|12.80|>
<|13.84|> For I have set the example that you should do as I have done to you.<|17.88|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Thank you.<|1.20|><|endoftext|>

<|17.92|> A pattern of life which is about being like Jesus and needing to imitate him.<|23.74|>

<|5.90|> Imitation involves humble acts of service that cannot be complicated or misunderstood<|11.48|>
<|11.48|> by doing them from a place of social status or power.<|16.20|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> In the act of foot washing, Christ sets aside his status as Lord and teacher and shows the<|6.04|>
<|6.04|> disciples that being a follower of Christ means that no act of love is beneath them.<|12.78|>
<|14.28|> The way of the towel is service over status.<|18.70|>
<|19.90|> It's humility over power.<|21.94|>
<|22.74|> And it's love over pride.<|24.82|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Thank you.<|1.28|><|endoftext|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> So I'd like to close this evening by asking us to think about Jesus' command to the disciples<|7.06|>

<|15.72|> you also ought to wash one another's feet.<|18.14|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> and<|1.06|><|endoftext|>

<|11.82|> and be willing to get your hands dirty<|14.06|>
